Facts.text: Julius Caesar's general Gaius Scribonius Curio is defeated in the Second Battle of the Bagradas River by the Numidians under Publius Attius Varus and King Juba I of Numidia. Curio commits suicide to avoid capture
K'an Ak (Precious Peccary) ascends to the throne of the Maya city of Tikal as its twelfth ruler, succeeding his father Sihyaj Chan K'awiil (Stormy Sky)
Battle of South Foreland: English fleet defeats France
Albert I of Habsburg is crowned King of Germany at Aachen Cathedral

Black Death Massacre: Jews of the Free Imperial City of Cologne set themselves on fire to avoid forced baptism into Christianity amidst Bubonic Plague accusations, part of a wave of pogroms across Western Europe
Jews of Palma, Mallorca, are massacred
The printing of the Gutenberg Bible is completed
Battle of Marj Dabiq: Ottoman forces decisively defeat the Mamluk Sultanate near Aleppo, ending Egyptian control in the Middle East
Conquistador Francisco de Orellana returns to Spain
First English East India convoy to the Mughal court, headed by William Hawkins, lands in Surat, India
The Great Colonial Hurricane affects the Virginia Colony at Jamestown
Job Charnock sets up the East India Company headquarters camp by Kalikata village (modern Kolkata)
Battle of Málaga: largest naval battle in the War of the Spanish Succession; tactically indecisive but a strategic victory for the Grand Alliance
Thomas Colley is executed in England for drowning a supposed witch
French Revolution: The National Assembly proclaims freedom of speech
British forces capture Washington, D.C. during the War of 1812 and destroy many landmarks
The Treaty of St. Louis is signed by the USA and the united tribes of Ottawa, Ojibwa, and Potawatomi in St. Louis, Missouri
Constitutionalist insurrection in Porto, Portugal
Netherlands' South Willems Port (Bosch-Liege) opens
Dutch army captures Fort Du Bus in New Guinea
HMS Beagle reaches Bahía Blanca, Argentina
First potato chips are prepared by chef George Crum at Moon's Lake House near Saratoga Springs, New York (popular legend says he invents them, though earlier recipes exist)
National Emigration Convention meets in Cleveland
The Panic of 1857 begins, setting off one of the most severe economic crises in U.S. history
Richmond "Daily Dispatch" reports 90 Black individuals arrested for learning
Liberia adopts a national flag designed and hand-stitched by a committee of seven women chaired by Susannah Elizabeth Lewis; all of the women are born in the US
American inventor Cornelius Swarthout patents a stovetop waffle iron
The Wolseley Expedition reaches Manitoba to end the Red River Rebellion
Riot abolishes fairs in Amsterdam; kills two people
American outlaw John Wesley Hardin, wanted for murder, is arrested by Texas Rangers on a train in Pensacola, Florida
Tornado destroys the coast of Savannah, Georgia, and Charleston, South Carolina; about 1,000 people die
Battle of Liao-Yang: 200,000 Japanese against 150,000 Russians; Japanese achieve a tactical victory
Chicago Cubs defeat the Phillies 2-1 in 20 innings
Cincinnati Red John Weimer no-hits Dodgers 1-0 in a 7-inning game
NY Giants scores are shown on electric diamonds known as "Compton's Baseball Bulletin" at Madison Square Garden
Workers start pouring concrete for the Panama Canal
Manuel d'Arriaga elected first president of Portugal
District of Alaska becomes an organized incorporated territory of the United States
US passes Anti-Gag Law, granting federal employees the right to petition the government
Battle of Bergen: Germans defeat Belgian and British troops
German troops occupy Namur, Belgium
Chicago Cubs win earliest pennant ever as season ends September 2
Frank King's daily comic strip "Gasoline Alley" is distributed nationwide by the Chicago Tribune Syndicate, appearing in The New York Daily News among other newspapers
Having pitched into the 9th inning, Cleveland's Ray Caldwell is flattened by a bolt of lightning and goes on to record the final out for the Indians' 2-1 win over the Philadelphia A's
With British approval, Greece is encouraged to take offensive action against Turkish nationalists in Asia Minor
Battle of Sakarya begins between Turkey and Greece and lasts for 21 days as Turkish forces eventually hold the final defensive line before Ankara
British airship R-38 crashes into the River Humber, killing 44 people
Palestinians attack Orthodox Jews in Jerusalem
Turkey and Persia sign a friendship treaty
The first Yser Tower ("Ijzertoren) is unveiled in Diksmuide, Belgium, as a memorial to the Flemish soldiers who lost their lives in the front line
France and the USSR sign a neutrality and non-aggression treaty
Australian Antarctic Territory is created
Basque Army surrenders to the Italian Corpo Truppe Volontarie following the Santoña Agreement during the Spanish Civil War
Republican offensive near Belchite, Spain
England beats Australia by an innings and 579 runs at The Oval
Virgil Trucks strikes out his 418th batter, the highest season total in organized baseball, for Andalusia in an Alabama-Florida League game
Germany and the USSR sign a 10-year non-aggression pact
WWII: German Luftwaffe bombs London
Sea battle off Eastern Solomon Islands between United States Navy and Imperial Japanese Navy
Transport #23 departs with French Jews to Nazi Germany
Philadelphia A's drop AL record-tying 20th game in a row, then win the second game
North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) goes into effect
Edith Sampson is named the first African American U.S. delegate to the UN
International Amateur Athletic Federation recognizes People's Republic of China
William Heatherton's "Reluctant Debutante" premieres in London
First non-stop transcontinental helicopter flight arrives in Washington, D.C.
At 17, future England soccer striker Jimmy Greaves scores on his First Division debut for Chelsea against Tottenham Hotspur in a 1–1 draw at White Hart Lane
Sergei Popov wins the Stockholm Marathon with a time of 2:15:17.0, setting a world record
England completes a 5-0 series sweep of India
Hiram L. Fong is sworn in as the first Chinese-American US Senator, while Daniel K. Inouye is sworn in as the first Japanese-American US Representative, both from Hawaii
-127°F (-88°C) recorded in Vostok, Antarctica; world record low temperature at the time, surpassed in 1983
Sixty people die when a bus plunges off a bridge into the Turvo River, Brazil
Former South African Nazi leader Johannes Vorster becomes South Africa's Minister of Justice
Windward Islands Airways International (Winair) forms
US performs a nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
24th Venice Film Festival: "Hands Over the City," directed by Francesco Rosi, wins the Golden Lion
First 200-meter freestyle swum in under 2 minutes by Don Schollander (1:58)
John Pennel is the first person to pole-vault 17 feet (5.2 m)
USSR launches Luna 11 into orbit around the Moon
France becomes the world's fifth thermonuclear power with a detonation at Mururoa Atoll in the South Pacific
Northern Ireland's first civil rights march is held; many more marches are held over the following year, and Loyalists organize counter-demonstrations to get the marches banned
Peru nationalizes US oil interests
Bomb kills one at University of Wisconsin's Army Math Research Center in Madison
India beats England by 4 wickets in their first win against the English
Dennis Amiss scores the first one-day international century, 103 against Australia
John Adams and his bass drum become a right-field fixture in Cleveland Stadium
Davey Lopes steals his 38th consecutive base, then is thrown out stealing
Papadopoulos, Pattakos, and Makarezos are sentenced to death in Athens
SF Giant Ed Halicki no-hits NY Mets 6-0
Tampa Bay Rowdies beat Portland 2-0 for the NASL Cup
Soyuz 21 returns to Earth
USSR performs an underground nuclear test
60,916 NFL fans choose the old Patriots logo over the new
Rock band "The Cars" plays a concert at Wollman Rink in NYC's Central Park
UN's Vienna office begins issuing postage stamps
Józef Pinkowski replaces Poland's Premier Babiuch
American Charles Chapman is the first Black person to swim the English Channel
KC's John Wathan steals his 31st base en route to 36 for a catcher's record
STS 51-I mission is scrubbed at T-5 minutes due to bad weather
American scientist Peter Schultz announces the possibility of Martian tornadoes
British brewery Bass buys Holiday Inn hotel chain
Pete Roseis suspended from Major League Baseball for life due to gambling
3,500 peacekeepers arrive in Liberia
Iraqi troops surround US and other embassies in Kuwait City
Sylvester Stallone wins the Cane Pace at Yonkers Raceway
Ukraine declares independence from the Soviet Union
Diplomatic relations are established between the People's Republic of China and South Korea
Hurricane Andrew hits South Florida, making landfall at Elliott Key and later Homestead; 44 people die, and $25 billion in damage is recorded
NFL Cleveland Browns suffer their worst pre-season loss, 56-3, to Minnesota Vikings
Screw magazine superimposes a gunsight over a picture of Larry Flynt
The first structural steel beams are erected at Jacobs Field (now Progressive Field) baseball stadium in Cleveland, Ohio
Padres score 14 in the first inning against Cardinals
Israel and the PLO initial an accord granting autonomy to Palestinians in the West Bank in education, health, taxation, social welfare, and tourism
Kieren Perkins swims a world record in the 1500 m freestyle (14:41.66)
Kieren Perkins swims a world record in the 800 m freestyle (7:46.00)
Fire that wipes out 6,000 acres begins in the Hamptons on Long Island
Microsoft debuts Windows 95
97th US Amateur Golf Championship won by Joel Kribel
Gordon Spence discovers 2^2976221 - 1 as the 36th known Mersenne prime
British Professor Kevin Warwick is the first human to receive an RIFD (radio-frequency identification) microchip implant to interact with electronic systems
The Netherlands is selected as the site for the trial of the two Libyan suspects of the 1988 Pan Am bombing
Argon fluorohydride, the first Argon compound ever known, is discovered at the University of Helsinki by Finnish scientists
Air Transat Flight 236 runs out of fuel over the Atlantic Ocean due to a fuel leak and makes a successful emergency landing in the Azores, saving all 306 people on board
Microsoft releases the software for Windows XP to manufacturing
Americans Kerri Walsh and Misty May team up to win the women's beach volleyball gold at the Athens Olympics beating Brazilian pair Shelda Bede & Adriana Behar 21–17, 21–11

Eighty-nine passengers die after two airliners explode after flying out of Domodedovo International Airport near Moscow. The explosions are caused by suicide bombers, reportedly female, from the Russian Republic of Chechnya.
Kenyan runners sweep the medals in the 3000 m steeplechase at the Athens Olympics; Ezekiel Kemboi wins gold ahead of Brimin Kipruto and Paul Kipsiele Koech
Moroccan superstar athlete Hicham El Guerrouj wins the first leg of his 1,500/5,000 m double at the Athens Olympics in 3:34.18
Roman Šebrle of the Czech Republic (8,893 points OR) edges American Bryan Clay (8,820) in the decathlon at the Athens Olympics
Yelena Isinbayeva of Russia vaults 4.91 m to break her own pole vault world record and wins the gold medal from teammate Svetlana Feofanova at the Athens Olympics
International Astronomical Union (IAU) redefines the term "planet," classifying Pluto as a dwarf planet
Dániel Varga and Péter Biros score 3 goals each as Hungary wins the men's water polo gold medal at the Beijing Olympics, their third consecutive Olympic gold medal

Samuel Wanjiru becomes the first Kenyan to win the men's Olympic marathon in Beijing, winning in an Olympic record time; silver medalist Jaouad Gharib of Morocco is also faster than the Olympic record, yet finishes 44 seconds behind
Summer Olympics in Beijing become the most-watched event on TV ever, with nearly 5 billion viewers, 70% of the world's population
XXIX Summer Olympic Games close at the Beijing National Stadium
"Love Like Crazy" single is released by Lee Brice (Billboard Song of the Year 2010)
Thailand's GDP grows 2.3% in the second quarter of 2009, technically leaving the recession
A US jury in California finds Samsung guilty of patent infringement and awards over US$1 billion in damages to Apple, while in a South Korean court, both are found guilty of patent infringement
Monsoon rains and floods kill 26 people in Pakistan
Yangmingtan Bridge collapses in China, killing three people
Four people are killed in a helicopter crash in the Shetland Islands
Thirty people are killed in a gang battle involving flamethrowers in Palmasola Prison, Bolivia
Nurse William Pooley flies back to the UK for emergency treatment after contracting the Ebola virus while attempting to treat patients in Sierra Leone
China stock market's "Black Monday" sees the Shanghai Composite lose 8.5%, sending other international markets lower
6.2-magnitude earthquake strikes central Italy, northeast of Rome, killing 268 and injuring 400
Astronomers announce the discovery of an Earth-like planet named Proxima b orbiting the star Proxima Centauri
Largest-ever lottery jackpot win in the US: $758.7 million won by Mavis Wanczyk of Massachusetts in the US Powerball Jackpot
Aerosmith lead singerSteven Tylersends a cease-and-desist letter to US President Trump, demanding he stop using the band's songs at rallies
Britain's Prince Andrew denies knowing his friend Jeffrey Epstein was involved in sexual trafficking of underage girls after public accusations made against him
NASA investigates the first possible crime in space as astronaut Anne McClain allegedly illegally accesses a bank account from space
US adventurer Victor Vescovo becomes the first person to visit the deepest point of every ocean when he reaches Molloy Hole in the Arctic
First documented case of a person being reinfected with COVID-19 a second time, a Hong Kong man four months after first infection
16th Paralympic Games opens in Tokyo, Japan
UN says Madagascar is on the brink of the world's first "climate change famine" with people suffering "catastrophic" levels of hunger after four years without rain
British-Belgian teen Mack Rutherford (17) becomes the youngest person to fly solo around the world, landing in Sofia, Bulgaria, after a five-month journey across 52 countries
South Korea records the world's lowest fertility rate at 0.81 (advanced economies average 1.6, population replacement needs 2.1)
BRICS meeting of emerging nations in South Africa agrees to invite six new nations to join in an attempt to balance the global power of the US and Europe
FIFA opens disciplinary case against Luis Rubiales, head of Spanish football, for kissing a player on the lips during celebrations for the Spanish women's World Cup win
Russian-French Telegram boss and founder Pavel Durov is arrested in France and later charged with failing to prevent illicit activity on the app
Seattle Mariners slugger Cal Raleigh hits two home runs, including his 49th, breaking MLB season record for a catcher in 11-4 win over visiting Oakland A's
Tour Championship Men's Golf, East Lake GC, GA: Englishman Tommy Fleetwood claims his first career PGA Tour win, beating Patrick Cantlay and Russell Henley by 3 strokes
Afonso de Albuquerqueof Portugal conquers Malacca, the capital of the Sultanate of Malacca
St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre of Protestants by Roman Catholics begins in Paris with the murder by defenestration of AdmiralGaspard de Colignyand later spreads to the French provinces
Duke of York,James II, awards the "Three Lower Counties" that form modern-day Delaware toWilliam Penn, ensuring ocean access for his colony of Pennsylvania
Wolfgang Amadeus Mozartcompletes his violin and piano sonata in A, K526
Naturalist John Henslow writes toCharles Darwinto offer him a place aboard the Admiralty surveying vessel HMS Beagle on a voyage to chart the South American coast
Thomas Edisonpatents the motion picture camera
Canadian championTommy BurnsKOs Bill Squires in the 13th round at Sydney Stadium, Australia, to retain the world heavyweight boxing title; Burns' third KO of Squires
Ticker-tape parade held in NYC forJim Thorpeand victorious US Olympians
Paul Rubens and Sidney Green's musical, with additional songs byJerome Kernand Herbert Reynolds, "The Girl from Utah" premieres at the Knickerbocker Theatre in NYC
Paavo Nurmiof Finland runs a world record 3-mile time of 14:11.2 in Stockholm during an intermediate time in a 5000 m event
Amelia Earhartbegins the first transcontinental non-stop flight by a woman in Los Angeles, California
US PresidentFranklin D. Rooseveltgives the FBI the authority to gather intelligence on fascist and communist groups
MLB Boston Red Sox left fielderTed Williamspitches the last two innings in a 12-1 loss to the Detroit Tigers, allowing three hits and one run at Fenway Park in Boston
FrenchGeneral Leclerc's troops open an assault on Paris
MLB Cleveland Indians aceBob Fellerreturns from serving in the US Navy and strikes out 12 batters
Bill Veeck's MLB St. Louis Browns hold "Grandstand Manager's Day" as coaches hold up placards for fans to vote on strategy options; Browns defeat visiting A's 5-3
US PresidentEisenhowersigns the Communist Control Act, outlawing the Communist Party at the height of McCarthyism
Los Angeles Dodgers coachLeo Durochersuffers a near-fatal allergic reaction to a penicillin injection while in the clubhouse at the Polo Grounds in NYC
Gordie HoweandJean Béliveauare inducted into the Hockey Hall of Fame
Musical film "Rock 'n' Roll High School," directed by Allan Arkush and featuring theRamones, premieres in NYC and is released throughout the US 20 days later
Mark David Chapman is sentenced to 20 years to life forJohn Lennon's murder
MLB Philadelphia Phillies'Pete Roseends his consecutive games played streak at 745
Pat Bradleysets an LPGA record for 9 holes with a 28 during the second round of the Columbia Savings National at Green Gables Country Club, Denver, CO
Mikhail Gorbachevresigns as head of the USSR Communist Party
Future 8-time Olympic gold medalistUsain Boltof Jamaica finishes 5th in his 200 m heat in 21.05 seconds, failing to qualify for the second round at the Athens Olympics
After signing a one-day contract with the San Francisco 49ers,Jerry Riceofficially retires from the NFL
The star-studded American men's basketball team, led byLeBron James, Chris Paul, andKobe Bryant, wins the gold medal at the Beijing Olympics with a relatively narrow 118-107 victory over Spain
Steve Jobsresigns as CEO of Apple Inc. and is succeeded byTim Cookdue to his illness
Facebook CEOMark Zuckerbergannounces that for the first time, 1 billion people log into Facebook
PhysicistStephen Hawkingpresents a new theory on black holes at a lecture at the KTH Royal Institute of Technology, Stockholm
Scott Morrisonbecomes Prime Minister of Australia after defeating Peter Dutton in a leadership spill, replacingMalcolm Turnbull
Brazilian PresidentJair Bolsonaroorders the army to help contain fires in the Amazon after widespread environmental destruction, wildlife loss and international criticism
Beyoncébecomes the first Black woman and the third person to wear the Tiffany Diamond in an advertising campaign for the company alongside her husband, Jay-Z
Kathy Hochulbecomes the first female Governor of New York, replacingAndrew Cuomoafter his resignation
US PresidentBidenannounces plan to cancel student loan debts by $10,000 (for those earning less than $125,000) and $20,000 for those who had received Pell grants
Former US PresidentDonald Trumpsurrenders at Fulton County Jail to be arrested on charges stemming from his attempts to alter the 2020 Georgia election and gets a mugshot
NorwegianMagnus Carlsendefeats Indian teenager Rameshbabu Praggnanandhaa in a tiebreak to win his first Chess World Cup championship

Believed until 2018 to be the date of the massive eruption of Mt. Vesuvius which buried the cities of Pompeii, Herculaneum, Oplontis and Stabiae, killing untold thousands, latest evidence suggests the eruption occurred after 17 October
Rome is overrun by Visigoths underAlaric Ifor the first time in nearly 800 years, seen as the fall of the Western Roman Empire
Pope Innocent III declares the Magna Carta invalid
Black Death Massacre: 6,000 Jews, blamed for spreading the Bubonic Plague, are killed in Mainz, Electorate of Mainz, as part of a wave of pogroms across Western Europe
Act of Uniformity requires English government and church officials to accept the Book of Common Prayer
Charlotte Brontësends the manuscript of her novel "Jane Eyre" to the publisher Smith, Elder & Co. in London under her pen name "Currer Bell"
Geoffrey Plantagenet, Count of Anjou, conquered Normandy, born in Le Mans, France (d. 1151)
King Alexander II of Scotland, born in Haddington, East Lothian, Scotland (d. 1249)
Floris IV, Count of Holland, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1234)
John I of Castile, King of Castile and León (1379-90), born in Épila, Spain (d. 1390)
Arthur III, Duke of Brittany, born in Château de Suscinio, Sarzeau, France (d. 1458)
Albrecht van Eyb, German humanist (Bamberg & Eichstattt), born in Ansbach, Germany (d. 1475)
Ercole Bottrigari, Italian composer, born in Bologna, Papal States (d. 1612)
Lavinia Fontana, Italian painter, born in Bologna, Papal States (now Italy) (d. 1614)
Bartholomeus Pitiscus, German mathematician (Trigonometry), born in Zielona Góra, Poland (d. 1613)
John Amner, English composer, born in Ely, Cambridgeshire, England (d. 1641)
John Taylor, English poet (An Arrant Thief), born in Gloucester, England (d. 1654)
Robert Herrick, English poet ('Gather ye rosebuds while ye may'), baptized in London (d. 1674)
Peder Griffenfeld, Danish statesman, born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1699)
Meinrad Spiess, German sacred music composer, Benedictine priest, and music theorist, born in Honsolgen, Electorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1761)
Theodor A Freiherr von Neuhoff, German adventurer and King of Corsica [or 8/25], born in Cologne, Germany (d. 1756)
Selina Hastings, Countess of Huntingdon, English evangelical revivalist, born in Ashby-de-la-Zouch, Leicestershire (d. 1791)
Cornelis Douwes, Dutch mathematician and astronomer, born in Terschelling, Netherlands (d. 1773)
Johann Balthasar Kehl, German composer, born in Coburg, Electorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1778)
David Traugott Nicolai, German composer, born in Görlitz, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1799)
Letizia Ramolino, Italian noble and mother of Napoleon Bonaparte, born in Ajaccio, France (d. 1836)
Sophia Frederica of Mecklenburg-Schwerin, Queen of Denmark and Norway, born in Schwerin, Germany (d. 1794)
William I, King of the Netherlands (1815-40), Prince of Orange and Grand Duke of Luxembourg, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1843)
James Weddell, British sailor and Antarctic explorer (Weddell Sea), born in Ostend, Belgium (d. 1834)
Thomas Fenwick Drayton, Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Charleston, South Carolina (d. 1891)
Daniel Gooch, English engineer, laid 1st successful transatlantic cables, born in Bedlington, Northumberland, England (d. 1889)
Aleksey Konstantinovich Tolstoy, Russian novelist, poet and playwright (Prince Serebrenni), born in Saint Petersburg, Russia (d. 1875)
Jacopo Tomadini, Italian composer, born in Cividale del Friuli, Lombardo-Venetian Kingdom (d. 1883)
Emanuele Muzio, Italian composer, born in Zibello,  Duchy of Parma and Piacenza (d. 1890)
Walter Husted Stevens, Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Penn Yan, New York (d. 1867)
George Hume "Maryland" Steuart, Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1903)
Theodore Dubois, French composer, born in Rosnay, Marne, France (d. 1924)
Eduard Napravnik, Czech conductor and composer, born in Býšť, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1916)
Edouard J. A. Agneessens, Flemish painter (Slave Market), born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1885)
James C. Calhoun, American soldier, brother-in-law of George Armstrong Custer, born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1876)
Charles Follen McKim, American Beaux-Arts architect (McKim Building of the Boston Public Library), born in Chester County, Pennsylvania (d. 1909)[1]
Tom Kendall, Australian cricket slow bowler (2 Tests, 14 wickets in Australia's 1st 2 Tests, BB 7/55), born in Bedford, England (d. 1924)
Nikolay Vladimirovich Scherbachov, Russian pianist and composer, born in St. Petersburg, Russia (d. 1922)
Felix Mottl, Austrian conductor and composer (Agnes Bernauer), born in Unter-St.-Veit, Austria (d. 1911)
Dragutin Lerman, Croatian explorer, born in Požega, Croatia (d. 1918)
Ferdinand I [Meinrad], King of Romania (1914-27), born in Sigmaringen, German Confederation (d. 1927)
Max Beerbohm, English caricaturist, writer and parodist (Saturday Review), born in London (d. 1956)
Rentaro Taki, Japanese pianist and composer, born in Tokyo, Japan (d. 1903)
André Cheron [Duval], French-American silent and sound screen character actor (The King of Kings; Anna Karenina; Wife vs. Secretary), born in Saint-Germain-en-Laye, France (d. 1952)
Miles Lampson, 1st Baron Killearn, British diplomat (High Commissioner of Egypt and the Sudan), born in Killearn, Scottland (d. 1964)
William Francis Gibbs, American naval architect, designed Liberty ships, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1967)
Harry Hooper, American Baseball HOF right fielder (World Series 1912, 15, 16, 18 Boston Red Sox), born in Bell Station, California (d. 1974)
Simon Kimbangu/Kibangu, Congolese religious leader and founder of Kimbanguism, born in Nkamba, Congo (d. 1951)
Duke Kahanamoku, American swimmer, surfer (Olympic gold 100m freestyle 1912, 20; 4x200m freestyle relay 1920), born in Honolulu, Hawaii (d. 1968)
Franz Philipp, German composer, born in Freiburg im Breisgau, Germany (d. 1972)
Jean Rhys [Ella Gwendolyn Rees Williams], British writer (Voyage in the Dark), born in Roseau, Dominica (d. 1979)
Alexander Mikhaylovich Dzegelyonok, Russian composer, born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1969)
Germaine Loosveldt, Flemish-Belgian stage and screen actress (Met den helm geboren), born in Halluin, France (d. 1960)
Elisha Scott, Irish soccer goalkeeper (31 caps Ireland [IFA]; Liverpool 430 games), born in Belfast, Ireland (d. 1959)
Richard Cardinal Cushing, Archbishop of Boston (1944-70), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1970)
Phil Baker, American comedian (Who's Whose), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1963)
Fred Rose, American songwriter and music publishing executive (Nashville's Acuff-Rose Music), born in Evansville, Indiana (d. 1954)
Albert Claude, Belgian biologist and physician (Nobel 1974), born in Longlier, Belgium (d. 1983)
Malcolm Cowley, American author (Flowering of New England), born in Belsano, Pennsylvania (d. 1989)
Johan Fabricius, Dutch novelist (Island of Demons) and adventurer, born in Bandung, Java, Indonesia (d. 1981)
Ruth Schaumann, German painter, writer and sculptor (Rose, Black King), born in Hamburg, Germany (d. 1975)
Preston Foster, American actor (Waterfront, Gunslinger), born in Ocean City, New Jersey (d. 1970)
Fernand Braudel, French historian (Civilization & Capitalism), born in Luméville, France (d. 1985)
Claude Hopkins, American jazz stride pianist, arranger, bandleader, and musical director (Josephine Baker), born in Alexandria, Virginia (d. 1984)
Karl Hanke, German Nazi Party (NSDAP) official and army officer (final Reichsführer of the Schutzstaffel (SS), 1945), born in Lubań, Poland (d. 1945)
(Henry) "Buster" Smith, American jazz alto saxophonist, arranger, and bandleader (Blue Devils; Count Basie), born in Alfdorf, Texas (d. 1991)
Rodd Redwing, American actor (Buffalo Bill In Tomahawk Territory; Son Of Geronimo), and stunt coach, born in New York City [or maybe India] (d. 1971)
Arthur "Big Boy" Crudup, American blues singer, songwriter and guitarist ("That's Alright"), born in Forest, Mississippi (d. 1974)
Don Douglas, Scottish actor (The Black Castle), born in Kinleyside, Scotland (d. 1945)
Siaka Stevens, President of Sierra Leone (1971-85), born in Moyamba, Sierra Leone (d. 1988)
Ronnie Grieveson, South African cricket wicket-keeper/batsman (2 Tests v Eng 1939), born in Johannesburg, Transvaal, South Africa (d. 1998)
Bernhard Heiden [Levi], German-American composer (Dreamers on a Slack Wire; Euphorion), and music teacher (University of Indiana, 1945-74), born in Frankfurt-am-Main, Germany (d. 2000)
Michel Pablo [Michalis N. Raptis], Chilean-Greek writer and Trotskyist revolutionary (Fourth International), born in Alexandria, Egypt (d. 1996)
Viktor Barna, Hungarian table tennis player (World C'ship gold singles 1930, 32, 33, 34, 35), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1972)
Durward Kirby, American TV announcer (Garry Moore Show), born in Indianapolis, Indiana (d. 2000)
Fritz "Freddie" Brocksieper, German big band jazz drummer, and bandleader, born in Constantinople, Turkey (d. 1990)
Dorothy Comingore [Linda Winters], American actress (Citizen Kane), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1971)
James Tiptree Jr. [Alice Sheldon], American sci-fi author (Byte Beautiful), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1987)
Ronald Ibbs, Irish actor (No Hiding Place, The Saint), born in Hendon, Middlesex, England (d. 1990)
Wynonie Harris, American rhythm and blues singer ("Who Threw The Whiskey in the Well"; "Good Rocking Tonight"), born in Omaha, Nebraska (d. 1969)
Anita V Figueredo, American physician and humanitarian, born in Costa Rica (d. 2010)
Hal Smith, American actor (The Andy Griffith Show-Otis Campbell), born in Petoskey, Michigan (d. 1994)
Léo Ferré, French chanson composer and singer ("Avec le temps", "Jolie Môme"), born in Monaco (d. 1993)
Dennis James, American wrestling announcer and TV host (PDQ), born in Jersey City, New Jersey (d. 1997)
Ray McIntire, American chemical engineer (invented Styrofoam at Dow), born in Gardner, Kansas (d. 1996)
Sikander Bakht, Indian politician (Governor of Kerala, 2002-04), born in Delhi, India (d. 2004)
"Drs P" [Heinz H Polzer], Swiss-Dutch songwriter and singer, born in Thun, Switzerland (d. 2015)
Bartolomé "Beny" Moré, Cuban guaracha and mambo singer, guitarist, songwriter, and bandleader, born in Santa Isabel de las Lajas, Cuba (d. 1963)
Enrique Llanes, Mexican professional wrestler, born in Otumba de Gómez Farías, Mexico (d. 2004)
Niels Viggo Bentzon, Danish pianist and composer (The Tempered Piano), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 2000)
Alex Colville, Canadian conceptual painter, born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 2013)
Sam Tingle, Zimbabwean racing driver, born in Manchester, England (d. 2008)
René Lévesque, Premier of Quebec, born in Campbellton, Canada (d. 1987)
Arthur Jensen, American psychologist, born in San Diego, California (d. 2012)
Helena Carter [Helen Rickerts], American model and actress (Invaders from Mars), born in New York City (d. 2000)
Louis Teicher, American pianist (Ferante & Teicher - "Exodus"), born in Wilkes-Barre, Pennsylvania (d. 2008)
